title: Human Trafficking
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Human Trafficking and Exploitation
summary: Scripture condemns the exploitation of human beings in the strongest terms, treating every person as an image-bearer of God whose dignity must be protected and whose freedom must be defended.
body:
The Bible's central narrative is a liberation story. God sees the suffering of his people enslaved in Egypt, hears their cry, and acts to set them free [verse:exod.3.7-8]. This is not background detail — it is the defining event of Israel's identity. A God who rescues the enslaved cannot be indifferent to modern slavery.

The Law explicitly prohibits kidnapping for exploitation. Whoever steals a person and sells them, or is found holding them, must be put to death [verse:exod.21.16]. Paul includes slave traders in his list of those whose behavior contradicts sound doctrine [verse:1tim.1.9-10]. The trafficking of human beings is categorically condemned.

Isaiah's vision of justice frames the mission in terms that speak directly to exploitation: to loose the bonds of wickedness, to undo the straps of the yoke, to let the oppressed go free [verse:isa.58.6]. Jesus echoes this at the beginning of his ministry, declaring that he has come to proclaim liberty to the captives [verse:luke.4.18].

Proverbs commands active intervention: rescue those being led away to death, and hold back those staggering toward slaughter [verse:prov.24.11-12]. The call is not merely to disapprove of trafficking but to act — to rescue, to intervene, to refuse complicity.

Every trafficked person bears the image of God [verse:gen.1.27]. Their exploitation is not merely a crime against them but an assault on the Creator whose likeness they carry.

verses.annotation: Kidnapping for exploitation carries the death penalty in the Mosaic law — the strongest possible condemnation.
True righteousness means loosing the bonds of oppression and letting the exploited go free.
God sees, hears, and acts on behalf of the enslaved — the Exodus as paradigm for liberation.
Paul includes slave traders among those whose behavior contradicts the gospel.
Jesus declares his mission as proclaiming freedom for captives.
A command to actively rescue those being led to death — silence and inaction are not options.
Every person bears God's image — exploitation of any person violates the Creator's design.

scholars.quote: Oswalt reads Isaiah 58:6 as God redefining worship — the most authentic act of devotion is the liberation of the oppressed, not ritual observance.

Towner notes that Paul's inclusion of slave traders in 1 Timothy 1 places trafficking alongside the most serious moral violations, indicating that the apostle viewed the commodification of human beings as fundamentally incompatible with the gospel.
scholars.source: The Letters to Timothy and Titus (Towner, 2006)

scholars.quote: Longman emphasizes that Proverbs 24:11-12 rejects passive awareness — knowing about injustice and failing to act is itself a moral failure that God will weigh.
